Front: What happens in the opening scene of Macbeth?
Back: The witches meet during a storm and plan to encounter Macbeth establishing the supernatural theme

Front: Why do the witches meet Macbeth?
Back: They plan to give prophecies that influence his ambition

Front: What prophecy do the witches give Macbeth?
Back: He will be Thane of Cawdor and later King of Scotland

Front: What prophecy do the witches give Banquo?
Back: His sons will become kings though he will not

Front: How does Macbeth become Thane of Cawdor?
Back: King Duncan rewards him for bravery in battle

Front: Why does Duncan visit Macbeth's castle?
Back: To honour Macbeth and celebrate his victory

Front: How does Lady Macbeth persuade Macbeth to kill Duncan?
Back: She questions his masculinity and manipulates his ambition

Front: How is King Duncan murdered?
Back: Macbeth stabs him while he sleeps

Front: Why do Malcolm and Donalbain flee?
Back: They fear being blamed for the murder

Front: Why does Macbeth become king?
Back: Duncan's sons flee making Macbeth the next in line

Front: Why does Macbeth fear Banquo?
Back: Because Banquo's sons are prophesied to become kings

Front: How does Macbeth arrange Banquo's murder?
Back: He hires murderers to kill Banquo and Fleance

Front: What happens at the banquet scene?
Back: Macbeth sees Banquo's ghost and begins to unravel

Front: Why does Macbeth visit the witches again?
Back: He seeks reassurance about his power

Front: What do the three apparitions show Macbeth?
Back: Warnings about Macduff and misleading prophecies about his safety

Front: Why does Macbeth kill Macduff's family?
Back: He wants revenge against Macduff

Front: What happens to Lady Macbeth later in the play?
Back: She sleepwalks due to guilt

Front: How does Macbeth react to Lady Macbeth's death?
Back: He reflects on the meaninglessness of life

Front: What happens in the final battle?
Back: Macduff leads the rebellion against Macbeth

Front: How does Macbeth die?
Back: Macduff kills him in battle

Front: Who is Macbeth?
Back: A Scottish nobleman and the tragic hero of the play

Front: What kind of character is Macbeth at the start?
Back: A brave and loyal soldier

Front: How does Macbeth change throughout the play?
Back: He becomes increasingly tyrannical and paranoid

Front: Why is Macbeth considered a tragic hero?
Back: He falls due to his fatal flaw ambition

Front: What is Macbeth's tragic flaw?
Back: His uncontrollable ambition

Front: Who is Lady Macbeth?
Back: Macbeth's ambitious and manipulative wife

Front: How does Lady Macbeth influence Macbeth?
Back: She pressures him to commit regicide

Front: Why does Lady Macbeth question Macbeth's masculinity?
Back: To manipulate him into committing murder

Front: How does Lady Macbeth show guilt?
Back: Through sleepwalking and hallucinations

Front: What happens to Lady Macbeth at the end of the play?
Back: She dies likely by suicide

Front: Who is Banquo?
Back: Macbeth's friend and fellow soldier

Front: How is Banquo different from Macbeth?
Back: He resists the witches' temptation

Front: Why is Banquo suspicious of Macbeth?
Back: He suspects Macbeth gained power through foul play

Front: Who is Fleance?
Back: Banquo's son

Front: Why is Fleance important?
Back: He represents the witches prophecy about Banquo's descendants

Front: Who is Macduff?
Back: A Scottish nobleman who opposes Macbeth

Front: Why does Macduff oppose Macbeth?
Back: He sees Macbeth as a tyrant

Front: What motivates Macduff to fight Macbeth?
Back: The murder of his family

Front: Who is King Duncan?
Back: The benevolent King of Scotland

Front: How is Duncan presented as a king?
Back: Kind generous and honourable

Front: Who is Malcolm?
Back: Duncan's son and rightful heir

Front: Why is Malcolm important at the end of the play?
Back: He restores order as king

Front: What role do the witches play in the play?
Back: They represent the supernatural and temptation

Front: How do the witches influence Macbeth?
Back: They spark his ambition with prophecy

Front: What is the theme of ambition in Macbeth?
Back: Ambition drives Macbeth to commit crimes

Front: What is the theme of power in Macbeth?
Back: Power corrupts those who pursue it ruthlessly

Front: What is the theme of guilt in Macbeth?
Back: Both Macbeth and Lady Macbeth suffer psychological torment

Front: What is appearance vs reality in Macbeth?
Back: Characters hide their true intentions

Front: What is fate vs free will in Macbeth?
Back: Debate over whether prophecy or choice controls events

Front: What role does the supernatural play?
Back: It manipulates and foreshadows events

Front: What is the theme of violence?
Back: Violence increases as Macbeth's tyranny grows

Front: What is the theme of masculinity?
Back: Characters question what it means to be a man

Front: What is the theme of loyalty?
Back: Many characters struggle between loyalty and morality

Front: What is the theme of betrayal?
Back: Macbeth betrays Duncan to gain power

Front: What is the theme of kingship?
Back: The play contrasts good and tyrannical rulers

Front: What is tyranny?
Back: Macbeth rules Scotland through fear

Front: What is order vs chaos?
Back: The natural order collapses after Duncan's murder

Front: What is the theme of madness?
Back: Guilt leads to psychological breakdown

Front: What is the theme of justice?
Back: Macbeth is punished for his crimes

Front: What is the theme of leadership?
Back: Malcolm represents rightful leadership

Front: What does the quote Fair is foul and foul is fair mean?
Back: It suggests that appearances can be deceptive

Front: What does the quote So foul and fair a day mean?
Back: Macbeth unknowingly echoes the witches language

Front: What does Vaulting ambition refer to?
Back: Macbeth recognises ambition as dangerous

Front: What does Stars hide your fires mean?
Back: Macbeth hides his dark desires

Front: What does Look like the innocent flower mean?
Back: Lady Macbeth advises deception

Front: What does Is this a dagger mean?
Back: Macbeth hallucinates due to guilt and conflict

Front: What does Neptune's ocean quote mean?
Back: Macbeth feels his guilt cannot be washed away

Front: What does A little water clears us mean?
Back: Lady Macbeth initially dismisses guilt

Front: What does Sleep no more mean?
Back: Macbeth has destroyed innocence

Front: What does Out damned spot mean?
Back: Lady Macbeth tries to wash imaginary blood

Front: What does What's done cannot be undone mean?
Back: Lady Macbeth realises consequences cannot be reversed

Front: What does Banquo's ghost represent?
Back: Macbeth's guilt and paranoia

Front: What does None of woman born mean?
Back: Macbeth believes he is invincible

Front: What does Birnam Wood prophecy mean?
Back: The witches give misleading prophecy

Front: What does Life's but a walking shadow mean?
Back: Macbeth sees life as meaningless

Front: What does Tomorrow and tomorrow mean?
Back: Macbeth reflects on life's futility

Front: What does Unsex me here mean?
Back: Lady Macbeth rejects traditional femininity

Front: What does I dare do all that may become a man mean?
Back: Macbeth struggles with masculinity

Front: What does When you durst do it mean?
Back: Lady Macbeth manipulates Macbeth's courage

Front: What does the blood symbolism represent?
Back: Guilt and violence

Front: What does darkness imagery represent?
Back: Evil secrecy and moral corruption

Front: What does light imagery represent?
Back: Goodness truth and innocence

Front: What does animal imagery represent?
Back: Chaos and unnatural behaviour

Front: What does the motif of sleep represent?
Back: Innocence and peace destroyed by guilt

Front: What does weather symbolism represent?
Back: Disorder in the natural world

Front: What is dramatic irony in Macbeth?
Back: The audience knows more than characters

Front: What is a soliloquy?
Back: A speech revealing a character's thoughts

Front: What is foreshadowing?
Back: Hints about future events

Front: What is juxtaposition?
Back: Contrasting ideas placed together

Front: What is a motif?
Back: A recurring idea or image

Front: What is symbolism?
Back: Objects representing deeper meanings

Front: What is tragic structure?
Back: A hero falls due to a fatal flaw

Front: What is a character foil?
Back: A character who contrasts another

Front: What is pathetic fallacy?
Back: Weather reflecting emotions

Front: What is metaphor?
Back: A comparison without like or as

Front: What is irony?
Back: A contrast between expectation and reality

Front: What is the Divine Right of Kings?
Back: The belief that kings are chosen by God

Front: What is the Great Chain of Being?
Back: A hierarchy of all living things in the universe

Front: Why was regicide feared?
Back: Killing a king was believed to disrupt divine order

Front: Why were witches feared in Jacobean times?
Back: They were believed to serve the devil

Front: Why is King James I important to Macbeth?
Back: He was fascinated by witchcraft

Front: What role did religion play in Jacobean society?
Back: It influenced moral beliefs about kingship

Front: Why would audiences fear the supernatural?
Back: They believed it could influence reality

Front: Why was loyalty to the king important?
Back: It maintained social order

Front: What is the moral lesson of Macbeth?
Back: Unchecked ambition leads to destruction

Front: Why is Macbeth still relevant today?
Back: It explores universal themes of power and ambition
